title: Assignment 1
text:
(b)
Multiple Choice Question
According to the speaker, why are simpler and interpretable models often preferred in many business applications?

Because they always give the most accurate results
Because they need very large datasets
Because decision makers need to understand the model results
Because they only work with structured data

Answer: C) Because decision makers need to understand the model results

title: Assignment 1
section: (c) Exploring Statistical Summaries with Custom Functions and Iteration Methods:
text:
(c) Exploring Statistical Summaries with Custom Functions and Iteration Methods:
In this section ‘mtcars’ data set is used, and a custom statistical summary function is defined.

#import mtcars dataset
data(mtcars)

#define a custom summary function
compute_stats <- function(x) {
  #check if data is numeric
  if (!is.numeric(x)) {
    stop("It must be a numeric vector.")
  }
  cars_stats_list <- list(mean = mean(x), 
                          median = median(x), 
                          variance = var(x), 
                          IQR = IQR(x), 
                          min = min(x), 
                          max = max(x))
  
  return(cars_stats_list)
}

cars_stats_hp <- compute_stats(mtcars$hp)
print(cars_stats_hp)

$mean
[1] 146.6875

$median
[1] 123

$variance
[1] 4700.867

$IQR
[1] 83.5

$min
[1] 52

$max
[1] 335


The “compute_stats” function is applied using for loop to calculate each data statistics.

#applying the function using for loop  
for (column in names(mtcars)) {
  cat("Summary for Column:", column,"\n")
  print(compute_stats(mtcars[[column]])) 
}

title: Assignment 1
text:
(d) Using data set polls_us_election_2016
The “polls_us_election_2016” data set from the dslabs package is used.

#install.packages("dslabs")
library(dslabs)
#import polls_us_election_2016 data set
data("polls_us_election_2016")

#define personel information varibles
my_first <- "Büşra"
my_birth_year <- 2000

#calculate integer k
k <- (nchar(my_first) + my_birth_year) %% 15 + 8
print(paste("The value of k:",k))

[1] "The value of k: 18"


k rows are displayed according to k value whether it is odd or even.

# Display k rows based on the value of k
if (k %% 2 == 0) {
  cat("k is even. The first", k, "rows of the data set are displayed below:\n")
  head(polls_us_election_2016, k)
  
} else {
  cat("k is odd. The last", k, "rows of the dataset are displayed below:\n")
  tail(polls_us_election_2016, k)
}

#number of NA values per column

print( sort_na_per_col)

           grade       samplesize            state        startdate 
             429                1                0                0 



#new object
replace_new <- polls_us_election_2016

#replace NA values according to column type. If it is numeric 2018, otherwise Büşra_18
for (column in colnames(replace_new)) {
  
  if (is.numeric(replace_new[[column]])) {
    replace_new[[column]][is.na(replace_new[[column]])] <- my_birth_year + k
    
  } else {
    replace_new[[column]] <- as.character(replace_new[[column]])
    replace_new[[column]][is.na(replace_new[[column]])] <- paste0(my_first, "_", k)
    
  }
}


#display k rows of the new data frame
if (k %% 2 == 0) {
  cat("k is even. The first", k, "rows of the new data set are displayed below:\n")
  head(replace_new, k)
  
} else {
  cat("k is odd. The last", k, "rows of the new data set are displayed below:\n")
  tail(replace_new, k)
  
}

title: Assignment 1
text:
(a) Video Summary
Video link: Veri Bilimi ve Endüstri Mühendisliği Üzerine Sohbetler – Mustafa Baydoğan & Erdi Daşdemir
In this video, Mustafa Baydoğan talks about the relationship between data science and industrial engineering and how data analysis methods are used to solve real-world problems. He explains that industrial engineers already work with tools like optimization, modeling, and system analysis, which are also essential in data science.
One of the main ideas in the talk is that before using machine learning or other data science techniques, it is important to first understand the real system and the problem itself. If the problem is not clearly defined, even very advanced models may give results that are not meaningful. Industrial engineers approach problems by analyzing the system, identifying key variables, and then building models that reflect the real situation.
The speaker also highlights real-world applications such as inventory management, demand forecasting in energy, e-commerce recommendation systems, and image-based predictions in manufacturing. These examples demonstrate that data science is not just about prediction but about supporting actionable decisions.
Another topic discussed in the video is the difference between traditional machine learning models and deep learning methods. Deep learning is especially useful when working with complex data like images, text, or audio. However, in many business problems, simpler and interpretable models are preferred because they are easier to understand and explain.
Overall, industrial engineers have an advantage in data science due to their ability to analyze systems, extract meaningful variables, and turn data into actionable decisions.

title: Assignment 1
text:
(b) Questions

Why is it important to understand the real system before applying machine learning or data science methods?

Answer: Understanding the system helps identify the real cause of the problem and the important variables. If we directly apply machine learning without understanding the problem, the model may find patterns in the data but those patterns might not represent the real situation. When the problem is clearly understood, data science methods can be used more effectively.

According to the speaker, why are simpler and interpretable models often preferred in many business applications?


Because they always give the most accurate results
Because they need very large datasets
Because decision makers need to understand the model results
Because they only work with structured data

Answer: C

(d) Displaying and Manipulating the ‘polls_us_election_2016’ Dataset
The ‘polls_us_election_2016’ data set from the ‘dslabs’ package is used.

#install.packages("dslabs")

#import polls_us_election_2016 data set

#define personnel information variables

#new object
replace_new <- polls_us_election_2016

#replace NA values according to column type. If numeric 2018, otherwise Büşra_18
for (column in colnames(replace_new)) {
  
  if (is.numeric(replace_new[[column]])) {
    replace_new[[column]][is.na(replace_new[[column]])] <- my_birth_year + k
    
  } else {
    replace_new[[column]] <- as.character(replace_new[[column]])
    replace_new[[column]][is.na(replace_new[[column]])] <- paste0(my_first, "_", k)
    
  }
}


#display k rows of the new data frame
if (k %% 2 == 0) {
  cat("k is even. The first", k, "rows of the new data set are displayed below:\n")
  head(replace_new, k)
  
} else {
  cat("k is odd. The last", k, "rows of the new data set are displayed below:\n")
  tail(replace_new, k)
  
}
